attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a six-hour history of diarrhoea, generalised weakness, and abdominal pain. Her medical history includes Hashimoto's thyroiditis, for which she takes thyroxine 100 mcg daily. She does not take any other regular medications.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 80/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 17/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and temperature 35.5°C. Her mucous membranes are dry, tongue is coated, and skin turgor reduced. Chest and abdominal examinations are unremarkable. Her neurological examination shows non-specific weakness, reflexes ++, normal coordination and sensation. Hyperpigmentation is seen in her palmar creases and at the site of a previous scar on her neck.

Her blood results show:

  • Potassium 4.9 mmol/L (3.5-4.9)

  • Sodium 135 mmol/L (137-145)

  • Chloride 115 mmol/L (100-109)

  • Magnesium 0.78 mmol/L (0.75-0.95)

  • Her blood glucose level on pinprick is 3.8 mmol/L (4-7.8).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her presentation?
attributes.explanation:
This presentation is consistent with an acute adrenal crisis, most likely due to primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease).

Primary adrenal insufficiency is a rare condition with an estimated prevalence of 4-11/100,000 and an incidence of 0.8/100,000 per year. In children, primary adrenal insufficiency is more common in males (75%), whereas in adults, the majority (70%) are female.

The underlying pathophysiology in the Western world is most commonly autoimmune adrenalitis (70-80% of cases), causing destruction of the adrenal gland leading to decreased secretion or absence of cortisol, with or without the reduction of aldosterone. This is most likely the case for this patient, who has another autoimmune disease, Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Worldwide however, infectious diseases such as tuberculosis, fungal infections and cytomegalovirus are important causes.

The presentation of primary adrenal insufficiency is variable and may be acute or chronic. Adrenal crises, as in the case presented, may present with any of the following - altered consciousness, circulatory collapse, hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia and seizures. An adrenal crisis may be precipitated by stress, sepsis, dehydration, trauma or rapid cessation of long term exogenous corticosteroids. Chronic primary adrenal insufficiency includes symptoms such as weakness, fatigue, weight loss, anorexia, nausea and vomiting, abdominal pain, constipation. Due to the excess ACTH seen in primary adrenal insufficiency, hyperpigmentation of the skin may also been seen, particularly in areas such as the axillae, nipples, pressure points and palmar creases.

Secondary adrenal insufficiency is due to hypopituitarism or a suppressed hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and characterised by a low plasma ACTH. Thus patients with secondary adrenal insufficiency will not have skin hyperpigmentation on examination.

Diabetic ketoacidosis typically presents with hyperglycaemia, not hypoglycaemia as seen in this patient. Additionally, DKA is characterized by metabolic acidosis with an increased anion gap, which is not evident in the laboratory values provided. The patient also lacks a history of diabetes.

Salicylate poisoning would typically present with tinnitus, hyperventilation, mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, and often vomiting. The patient's presentation with hypotension, hyperpigmentation, and borderline hypoglycaemia is not consistent with salicylate toxicity.

Myxoedema (severe hypothyroidism) can present with bradycardia, hypothermia, and altered mental status, but this patient is already on thyroxine replacement for Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Additionally, myxoedema would not explain the hyperpigmentation or the specific pattern of electrolyte abnormalities seen here.

Starvation can cause weakness and hypoglycaemia but would not typically cause the hyperpigmentation seen in this patient. Additionally, starvation alone would not explain the acute presentation with hypotension and tachycardia suggestive of an adrenal crisis.

Adrenal crisis is a medical emergency and requires immediate resuscitation and restoration of circulatory volume and administration of IV hydrocortisone. In patients with subacute or chronic disease, management will focus on monitoring of electrolyte disturbances, steroid replacement and patient education. Early referral to an endocrinologist will be necessary for further investigations to confirm primary adrenal insufficiency.
learningPoints: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) presents with hypotension, weakness, hyperpigmentation, and electrolyte abnormalities.
Autoimmune adrenalitis is the most common cause of primary adrenal insufficiency in Western countries.
An adrenal crisis requires prompt resuscitation with intravenous fluids and hydrocortisone.
references: Huecker MR, Bhutta BS, Dominique E. Adrenal insufficiency. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan–. [Updated 2023 Aug 17; cited 2025 Jun 25]. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441832/
O’Connell S, Siafarikas A. Addison disease Diagnosis and initial management. Australian Family Physician. 2010;39:834-7. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2010/november/addison-disease
